hq: 1111B S Governors Ave Unit 28280, Dover, DE 19904, United States

system_description.overview: ThriveAI is a SaaS platform operating as an AI-powered product manager. It connects to customer-selected product information sources (product metrics, user feedback, internal documentation) and uses AI-driven analysis to proactively flag emerging issues, surface insights, and provide on-demand support for product decision-making and prioritization.

products.ThriveAI SaaS Platform: Web-based application for product teams with data ingestion/integration connectors, AI-driven analysis for insights/trends/alerts, proactive monitoring and alerting, on-demand natural-language product management assistance, and administrative capabilities for user/account management

regions: Not explicitly named; GCP multi-AZ mentioned
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices; all data processed on cloud
additional_cloud: Neon PostgreSQL (AWS us-east-2, carve-out external service)
infrastructure.cdn: Google-managed SSL Certificate / GCP Global Load Balancer (static IP)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPCs with Access Control Lists (ACLs). Customer access segmented from other customer accounts. Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.waf: Web Application Firewall (WAF) via GCP — visible in network diagram as 'WebSocket (WSS)' entry point
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via GCP to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings
network_architecture.vpn: Bastion host with VPN for remote access
network_architecture.tls: TLS (Transport Layer Security) for all web-based communications; certificates from established third-party CA
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; all systems restricted to internet via proxy
network_architecture.topology: Internet traffic enters via HTTPS to GCP Global Load Balancer (Static IP) -> Google-managed SSL Certificate -> GCP AutoPilot Cluster: Back App + Webapp (Next.JS) via internal HTTP -> API Server (FastAPI) -> Data Integration Platform / Job Workers / Analytics Database -> Cloud Storage + Memorystore (Redis). External services: Neon PostgreSQL (AWS us-east-2) and LLM Providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)

application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments; changes tested before deployment to Stage or Production

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: GCP (password policy via GCP); MFA enforced for production platform
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production platform; also enforced for source code management tool access and remote access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts; no guest/anonymous logins; unattended desktops locked on inactivity

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service (GCP KMS implied)
encryption.key_management: GCP cloud provider key management service; access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool (name not disclosed; MFA-protected, branch rules enforced)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to move change to production environment
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration as code — baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO; monthly patch management reviews; workstations configured for automatic updates; daily patch compliance scans; OS patches applied as available

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named — monitoring tool generates alerts on predefined thresholds; tracked in change-management/ticketing application
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: GCP logging (implied); logs cover administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts to IT management for high and critical risk events; incident management process invoked for confirmed events

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure, compute, storage, networking, data center services
LLM provider for AI analysis and features
LLM provider for AI analysis and features
Additional LLM providers shown in network diagram

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed on all production servers and Mac workstations/laptops; definitions updated weekly on servers, daily automatic scans; endpoint security solution with automatic scanning
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; scan frequency adjustable; remediation plan for critical/high vulnerabilities
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party

untestable: CC2.2.3 — no significant changes to people/roles during engagement period
CC3.2.1 — cybersecurity insurance control could not be tested because no cyber incidents reported during engagement period
CC6.5.2 — customer data deletion control could not be tested because no customer terminations during engagement period
CC7.3.1 — security incident response control could not be tested because no security incidents reported during engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — GCP responsible for physical security of data center; physical security controls not in scope
A1.2 — Thrive AI does not have an office; environmental security controls managed by building management/GCP
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust services categories determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: MFA enforced for all administrative access to production platform AND source code tool
Quarterly user access reviews for all user accounts including administrative privilege accounts
Access termination within one business day
Annual external penetration testing by third party
Monthly vulnerability scans with automated tooling
BCDR tested at least annually per loss scenario schedule
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed
Risk and Governance Executive Committee (RGEC) meets semiannually; IT Leadership Committee meets weekly

infrastructure.rationale: GCP multi-AZ with Kubernetes AutoPilot, IaC-based configuration management, VPC segmentation, WAF, IDS/IPS, bastion/VPN. No multi-region. External PostgreSQL dependency on Neon (AWS) adds cross-cloud complexity. CDN via GCP Global Load Balancer.

application_architecture.rationale: Microservices on GCP with Next.JS frontend, FastAPI backend, dedicated Job Workers and Analytics DB layer. Separate environments, branch protection with required approvals. Language stack not fully named beyond these two frameworks. No mention of automated testing coverage.

data_layer.rationale: Multi-database approach (Neon PostgreSQL + Analytics DB), Redis caching, GCP Cloud Storage, daily backups with multi-AZ. Data classification and retention policies in place. Per-tenant segregation confirmed. No explicit data warehouse named.

security_posture.rationale: Strong posture: WAF + IDS/IPS + firewall + annual external pen test + monthly vuln scans + endpoint protection + antivirus + MFA enforced across production and SCM + cybersecurity insurance. Log protection and automated alerting in place. No named SIEM tool.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC confirmed, GCP Kubernetes AutoPilot, branch protection with required approvals, separate environments, emergency change process, automatic patch management. CI/CD tooling not named. Source control tool not named.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually, annual testing per loss scenario schedule, daily backups with multi-AZ, multi-AZ failover capability via GCP. No RTO/RPO disclosed. No multi-region failover.

vendor_diversity.rationale: 5+ vendors identified (GCP, Neon/AWS, OpenAI, Anthropic, others). Primary compute on GCP (high concentration). Critical database dependency on Neon (AWS, external). LLM multi-provider is a strength. LLM dependency overall is a risk for AI product.

overall.rationale: Solid early-stage SaaS security posture on GCP with Kubernetes microservices, strong access controls (MFA, quarterly reviews, 1-day revocation SLA), annual pen testing, and monthly vuln scans. Good network diagram transparency. Gaps: RTO/RPO not disclosed, CI/CD tooling unnamed, SIEM not named, single-region deployment, cross-cloud DB dependency on Neon (AWS).

red_flags: RTO and RPO not disclosed — no quantified recovery commitments
Primary database (Neon PostgreSQL) hosted on AWS us-east-2 — cross-cloud dependency outside primary GCP environment
CI/CD tool and source control platform not named — limits auditability transparency
No multi-region failover — single GCP region deployment
yellow_flags: LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic) are critical dependencies for core product functionality — subject to third-party availability and policy changes
No SIEM named — logging infrastructure relies on unspecified tooling
APM tool unnamed — monitoring system identity not disclosed
3-month audit period (Sep-Dec 2025) is short — limited operating effectiveness evidence window
Cybersecurity insurance control could not be tested (no incidents during period) — not a finding but noted as untestable
green_flags: Network diagram included in report showing full GCP production architecture with named components
MFA enforced for all production admin access AND source code management tool
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Access revocation within one business day of termination
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation tracking
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration tested
BCDR tested annually per documented loss scenario schedule
Zero exceptions across all 52 tested controls
Content filtering via proxy server for all internet traffic
Endpoint protection on all production servers and Mac workstations
IaC-based configuration management with rollback capability
key_observations: Thrive AI uses a cross-cloud architecture: primary GCP for compute/hosting but Neon (an AWS-hosted managed PostgreSQL) as the primary database — this is an unusual dependency that creates cross-cloud latency and availability coupling

The network diagram explicitly shows LLM Providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) as external services in the production flow — the AI product is tightly coupled to third-party LLM APIs, making LLM provider uptime/policy a first-order business risk
FastAPI backend suggests Python-first server-side architecture, consistent with an AI-heavy product stack
GCP AutoPilot (serverless Kubernetes) is used — this means Google manages the node infrastructure, reducing operational overhead but also reducing direct infrastructure visibility
The report covers only Security trust criteria — Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy were not tested
